title: Jay-Z

infobox.value: Shawn Corey Carter   (1969-12-04) December 4, 1969 (age 56)  New York City, U.S.
* The Carter Administration * Jigga * Hova * El Presidente * HOV[[1]](#cite_note-1)
* Rapper * songwriter * record producer * record executive * businessman * media proprietor * actor

* [Albums](/wiki/Jay-Z_albums_discography "Jay-Z albums discography") * [production](/wiki/Jay-Z_singles_discography#Production_discography "Jay-Z singles discography") * [singles](/wiki/Jay-Z_singles_discography "Jay-Z singles discography") * [songs](/wiki/List_of_songs_recorded_by_Jay-Z "List of songs recorded by Jay-Z") * [videography](/wiki/Jay-Z_videography "Jay-Z videography")
[Full list](/wiki/List_of_awards_and_nominations_received_by_Jay-Z "List of awards and nominations received by Jay-Z")

* [Roc Nation](/wiki/Roc_Nation "Roc Nation") * [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Records "Atlantic Records") * [Def Jam](/wiki/Def_Jam_Recordings "Def Jam Recordings") * [Roc-A-Fella](/wiki/Roc-A-Fella_Records "Roc-A-Fella Records") * [NorthWestSide](/wiki/Arista_Records "Arista Records")[[5]](#cite_note-5) * [Priority](/wiki/Priority_Records "Priority Records") * [Payday](/wiki/Payday_Records "Payday Records") * [FFRR](/wiki/FFRR_Records "FFRR Records")
* [The Commission](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G.") * [High Potent](/wiki/Jaz-O "Jaz-O") * [Murder Inc.](/wiki/Murder_Inc._(rap_group) "Murder Inc. (rap group)")

(born December 4, 1969), known professionally as **Jay-Z**,[[a]](#cite_note-8) is an American rapper, businessman, and record executive. He was named [the greatest rapper of all time](/wiki/Billboard_and_Vibe%27s_50_Greatest_Rappers_of_All_Time "Billboard and Vibe's 50 Greatest Rappers of All Time") by *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* and *[Vibe](/wiki/Vibe_(magazine) "Vibe (magazine)")* in 2023.[[8]](#cite_note-:6-9) Rooted in [East Coast hip-hop](/wiki/East_Coast_hip-hop "East Coast hip-hop"), Jay-Z is known for his complex lyricism that often uses [double entendres](/wiki/Double_entendre "Double entendre"), [wordplay](/wiki/Wordplay "Wordplay"), and [braggadocio](/wiki/Braggadocio_(rap) "Braggadocio (rap)"). His music is built on a [rags to riches](/wiki/Rags_to_riches "Rags to riches") narrative. He is the [wealthiest musical artist in history](/wiki/List_of_music_artists_by_net_worth "List of music artists by net worth"), worth US$2.8 billion as of 2026.[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)

**Shawn Corey Carter** (born December 4, 1969), known professionally as **Jay-Z**,[[a]](#cite_note-8) is an American rapper, businessman, and record executive. He was named [the greatest rapper of all time](/wiki/Billboard_and_Vibe%27s_50_Greatest_Rappers_of_All_Time "Billboard and Vibe's 50 Greatest Rappers of All Time") by *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* and *[Vibe](/wiki/Vibe_(magazine) "Vibe (magazine)")* in 2023.[[8]](#cite_note-:6-9) Rooted in [East Coast hip-hop](/wiki/East_Coast_hip-hop "East Coast hip-hop"), Jay-Z is known for his complex lyricism that often uses [double entendres](/wiki/Double_entendre "Double entendre"), [wordplay](/wiki/Wordplay "Wordplay"), and [braggadocio](/wiki/Braggadocio_(rap) "Braggadocio (rap)"). His music is built on a [rags to riches](/wiki/Rags_to_riches "Rags to riches") narrative. He is the [wealthiest musical artist in history](/wiki/List_of_music_artists_by_net_worth "List of music artists by net worth"), worth US$2.8 billion as of 2026.[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)

A protégé of fellow New York City-based rapper [Jaz-O](/wiki/Jaz-O "Jaz-O"), Jay-Z began recording music in the late 1980s. He co-founded [Roc-A-Fella Records](/wiki/Roc-A-Fella_Records "Roc-A-Fella Records") in 1994 to release his first two studio albums, *[Reasonable Doubt](/wiki/Reasonable_Doubt_(album) "Reasonable Doubt (album)")* (1996) and *[In My Lifetime, Vol. 1](/wiki/In_My_Lifetime,_Vol._1 "In My Lifetime, Vol. 1")* (1997), both of which received acclaim. His 11 subsequent albums, including *[The Blueprint](/wiki/The_Blueprint "The Blueprint")* (2001), *[The Black Album](/wiki/The_Black_Album_(Jay-Z_album) "The Black Album (Jay-Z album)")* (2003), *[American Gangster](/wiki/American_Gangster_(album) "American Gangster (album)")* (2007), and *[4:44](/wiki/4:44 "4:44")* (2017), have each debuted atop the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"). Jay-Z has a total of 14 number-one [albums](/wiki/Jay-Z_albums_discography "Jay-Z albums discography") on the chart, the [joint-third most in history](/wiki/Billboard_200#Artist_milestones "Billboard 200"). He topped the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") on four occasions: once as a lead artist with his 2009 single "[Empire State of Mind](/wiki/Empire_State_of_Mind "Empire State of Mind")" (featuring [Alicia Keys](/wiki/Alicia_Keys "Alicia Keys")), and three times with his guest performances on the singles "[Heartbreaker](/wiki/Heartbreaker_(Mariah_Carey_song) "Heartbreaker (Mariah Carey song)")" (1999) by [Mariah Carey](/wiki/Mariah_Carey "Mariah Carey"), "[Crazy in Love](/wiki/Crazy_in_Love "Crazy in Love")" (2003) by [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé"), and "[Umbrella](/wiki/Umbrella_(song) "Umbrella (song)")" (2007) by [Rihanna](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna").[[11]](#cite_note-12)

Jay-Z served as president and chief executive officer of [Def Jam Recordings](/wiki/Def_Jam_Recordings "Def Jam Recordings") from 2004 to 2007,[[12]](#cite_note-13) during which he helped launch the careers of successful artists including [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West"), Rihanna, and [Ne-Yo](/wiki/Ne-Yo "Ne-Yo"). He co-founded [Rocawear](/wiki/Rocawear "Rocawear"), a clothing retailer, in 1999,[[13]](#cite_note-Luxury-14) and founded the [40/40 Club](/wiki/40/40_Club_(venue) "40/40 Club (venue)"), a luxury bar chain, in 2003. As both grew into multi-million-dollar businesses, he launched [Roc Nation](/wiki/Roc_Nation "Roc Nation"), a multi-disciplinary entertainment agency, in 2008. In 2015, he acquired the technology company [Aspiro](/wiki/Aspiro "Aspiro") and led the expansion of its media streaming service [Tidal](/wiki/Tidal_(service) "Tidal (service)").[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17) Through his business ventures, Jay-Z became the first [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") [billionaire](/wiki/Black_billionaire "Black billionaire") in 2019.[[17]](#cite_note-networth-18)[[18]](#cite_note-Billionaire-19) He married Beyoncé in 2008; they have three children.

One of the [best-selling music artists](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_music_artists "List of best-selling music artists") with 140 million records sold, Jay-Z has won 25 [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards"), the [eighth-most of all time](/wiki/Grammy_Award_records#Most_Grammys_won "Grammy Award records") and the second-most of any hip-hop artist.[[19]](#cite_note-20) He is the recipient of the [NAACP](/wiki/NAACP "NAACP")'s [President's Award](/wiki/NAACP_Image_Award_%E2%80%93_President%27s_Award "NAACP Image Award – President's Award") and three [Emmy Awards](/wiki/Emmy_Awards "Emmy Awards") (including two [Primetime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Awards "Primetime Emmy Awards")), in addition to being nominated for a [Tony Award](/wiki/Tony_Awards "Tony Awards"). Ranked by *Billboard* as the [highest-grossing American hip-hop touring act](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_live_music_artists#Rankings_by_category "List of highest-grossing live music artists"), Jay-Z was the first rapper to be inducted into the [Songwriters Hall of Fame](/wiki/Songwriters_Hall_of_Fame "Songwriters Hall of Fame")[[20]](#cite_note-21) and the first solo living rapper inducted in the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame"). *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* named him one of the [100 most influential people in the world](/wiki/Time_100 "Time 100") in 2013.[[21]](#cite_note-:3-22)

## Early life and education

Shawn Corey Carter was born on December 4, 1969, in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), New York City.[[22]](#cite_note-23) He was raised in [Marcy Houses](/wiki/Marcy_Houses "Marcy Houses"), a public housing project in Brooklyn's [Bedford–Stuyvesant](/wiki/Bedford%E2%80%93Stuyvesant,_Brooklyn "Bedford–Stuyvesant, Brooklyn") neighborhood.[[23]](#cite_note-allmusicbio-24) His father, Adnis Reeves,[[24]](#cite_note-25) abandoned the family when Jay-Z was 11 years old, and Jay-Z and his three older siblings were raised by their mother, Gloria Carter. Reeves later met and reconciled with Jay-Z prior to his death in 2003. Jay-Z claims in his lyrics to "You Must Love Me", the closing track on his 1997 album *In My Lifetime, Vol. 1*, that in 1982, at age 12, he shot his older brother Eric in the shoulder for stealing his jewelry.[[25]](#cite_note-CBSnews-26)[[26]](#cite_note-aboutjayz-27)

He attended Eli Whitney High School and [George Westinghouse Career and Technical Education High School](/wiki/George_Westinghouse_Career_and_Technical_Education_High_School "George Westinghouse Career and Technical Education High School"), both in Brooklyn, and then [Trenton Central High School](/wiki/Trenton_Central_High_School "Trenton Central High School") in [Trenton, New Jersey](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey"). He did not graduate, dropping out during his sophomore year at Trenton Central High School.[[27]](#cite_note-28) According to his interviews and lyrics, he sold [crack cocaine](/wiki/Crack_cocaine "Crack cocaine")[[26]](#cite_note-aboutjayz-27) and was shot at three times during this period.[[25]](#cite_note-CBSnews-26) His former friend was sentenced to prison for possessing drugs and weapons.[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30) Known as "Jazzy" around the neighborhood, he later adopted the stage name "Jay-Z" in homage to his mentor [Jaz-O](/wiki/Jaz-O "Jaz-O").[[23]](#cite_note-allmusicbio-24)[[30]](#cite_note-hyphen-31)

## Career

### 1986–1995: Early career and recordings

Jay-Z can be briefly heard on several of Jaz-O's early recordings in the late 1980s and early 1990s, including "[H. P. Gets Busy](/wiki/H._P._Gets_Busy "H. P. Gets Busy")", "The Originators" and "Hawaiian Sophie".[[31]](#cite_note-EdwardsRap14-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33) Jaz-O's record label came up with the idea to create an album with a concept reminiscent of [DJ Jazzy Jeff & the Fresh Prince](/wiki/DJ_Jazzy_Jeff_%26_the_Fresh_Prince "DJ Jazzy Jeff & the Fresh Prince"), featuring Jaz-O as the rapper, Jay-Z as the [hype man](/wiki/Hype_man "Hype man"), and [Irv Gotti](/wiki/Irv_Gotti "Irv Gotti") as the DJ. While working on the album *[Word to the Jaz](/wiki/Word_to_the_Jaz "Word to the Jaz")* in London in 1988, Jay-Z and Gotti began to build their relationship on the trip.[[33]](#cite_note-m750-34)[[34]](#cite_note-a513-35)[[35]](#cite_note-y078-36)[[36]](#cite_note-y360-37)[[37]](#cite_note-r035-38) Jay-Z became embroiled in several battles with rapper [LL Cool J](/wiki/LL_Cool_J "LL Cool J") in the early 1990s.[[38]](#cite_note-39) He first became known to a wide audience on the [posse cut](/wiki/Posse_cut "Posse cut") "Show and Prove" on the 1994 [Big Daddy Kane](/wiki/Big_Daddy_Kane "Big Daddy Kane") album *[Daddy's Home](/wiki/Daddy%27s_Home_(Big_Daddy_Kane_album) "Daddy's Home (Big Daddy Kane album)").* Jay-Z has been referred to as Big Daddy Kane's [hype man](/wiki/Hype_man "Hype man") during this period,[[39]](#cite_note-EdwardsRap316-40) although Kane explains that he did not fill the traditional hype man role, and was instead basically making cameo appearances on stage. "When I would leave the stage to go change outfits, I would bring out Jay-Z and [Positive K](/wiki/Positive_K "Positive K") and let them freestyle until I came back to the stage."[[40]](#cite_note-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42)

According to his second verse on "[99 Problems](/wiki/99_Problems "99 Problems")", released in 2003, Jay-Z was allegedly stopped by an NYPD detective in 1994 while en route to [I-95](/wiki/Interstate_95 "Interstate 95"), possibly for a search of drugs in his car. [Detection dogs](/wiki/Detection_dogs "Detection dogs") were called, but another police car had passed; Jay-Z was let go soon after.[[42]](#cite_note-43) Jay-Z appeared on a popular song by [Big L](/wiki/Big_L "Big L"), "Da Graveyard", and on [Mic Geronimo](/wiki/Mic_Geronimo "Mic Geronimo")'s "Time to Build", which also featured early appearances by his former [Murder Inc.](/wiki/Murder_Inc._(rap_group) "Murder Inc. (rap group)") colleagues [Ja Rule](/wiki/Ja_Rule "Ja Rule") and [DMX](/wiki/DMX "DMX") in 1995. His first official rap single was "[In My Lifetime](/wiki/In_My_Lifetime_(song) "In My Lifetime (song)")", which was released with an accompanying music video in 1995. An unreleased music video was also produced for the B-side "I Can't Get with That".[[43]](#cite_note-44)

### 1995–2000: *Reasonable Doubt*, *In My Lifetime, Vol. 1*, *Vol. 2...*, *Vol. 3...*, and *The Dynasty*

With no major label to give him a record deal, Jay-Z sold [burned CDs](/wiki/CD-R "CD-R") out of his car,[[25]](#cite_note-CBSnews-26) and with [Damon "Dame" Dash](/wiki/Damon_Dash "Damon Dash") and [Kareem "Biggs" Burke](/wiki/Kareem_Burke "Kareem Burke"), created [Roc-A-Fella Records](/wiki/Roc-A-Fella_Records "Roc-A-Fella Records") as an independent label in 1995. After striking a distribution deal with [Priority](/wiki/Priority_Records "Priority Records"), Jay-Z released his 1996 debut album *[Reasonable Doubt](/wiki/Reasonable_Doubt_(album) "Reasonable Doubt (album)")* with [beats](/wiki/Hip_hop_production "Hip hop production") from acclaimed producers such as [DJ Premier](/wiki/DJ_Premier "DJ Premier") and [Super DJ Clark Kent](/wiki/DJ_Clark_Kent "DJ Clark Kent") and an appearance by [The Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G.") The album reached number 23 on the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") and went gold that year.[[23]](#cite_note-allmusicbio-24) After reaching a new label distribution deal with [Def Jam](/wiki/Def_Jam_Recordings "Def Jam Recordings") in 1997, Jay-Z released his follow-up *[In My Lifetime, Vol. 1](/wiki/In_My_Lifetime,_Vol._1 "In My Lifetime, Vol. 1").* Featuring production by [Sean Combs](/wiki/Sean_Combs "Sean Combs"), DJ Premier, and [Ski](/wiki/Ski_Beatz "Ski Beatz"), it earned platinum status in the United States.[[44]](#cite_note-riaacertification-45)

In 1998, Jay-Z released *[Vol. 2... Hard Knock Life](/wiki/Vol._2..._Hard_Knock_Life "Vol. 2... Hard Knock Life")* which spawned the biggest hit of his career at the time, "[Hard Knock Life (Ghetto Anthem)](/wiki/Hard_Knock_Life_(Ghetto_Anthem) "Hard Knock Life (Ghetto Anthem)")". He relied more on [flow](/wiki/Flow_(rapping) "Flow (rapping)") and wordplay, and he continued with his penchant for mining beats from the popular producers of the day such as [Swizz Beatz](/wiki/Swizz_Beatz "Swizz Beatz"), an upstart in-house producer for [Ruff Ryders](/wiki/Ruff_Ryders "Ruff Ryders"), and [Timbaland](/wiki/Timbaland "Timbaland").[[45]](#cite_note-46) Other producers included [DJ Premier](/wiki/DJ_Premier "DJ Premier"), [Erick Sermon](/wiki/Erick_Sermon "Erick Sermon"), [The 45 King](/wiki/The_45_King "The 45 King"), and [Kid Capri](/wiki/Kid_Capri "Kid Capri"). Charting hits from this album included "[Can I Get A...](/wiki/Can_I_Get_A... "Can I Get A...")", featuring [Ja Rule](/wiki/Ja_Rule "Ja Rule") and [Amil](/wiki/Amil_(rapper) "Amil (rapper)"), and "[Nigga What, Nigga Who](/wiki/Nigga_What,_Nigga_Who_(Originator_99) "Nigga What, Nigga Who (Originator 99)")", featuring Amil and [Jaz-O](/wiki/Jaz-O "Jaz-O"). *Vol. 2* would eventually become Jay-Z's most commercially successful album; it was certified 5× Platinum in the United States and has to date sold over five million copies.[[44]](#cite_note-riaacertification-45) The album went on to win a Grammy Award, although Jay-Z boycotted the ceremony protesting DMX's failure to garner a Grammy nomination and the academy's decision to not broadcast urban music categories.[[46]](#cite_note-47)
In 1999, Jay-Z collaborated with [Mariah Carey](/wiki/Mariah_Carey "Mariah Carey") on "[Heartbreaker](/wiki/Heartbreaker_(Mariah_Carey_song) "Heartbreaker (Mariah Carey song)")", a song from her seventh album, *[Rainbow](/wiki/Rainbow_(Mariah_Carey_album) "Rainbow (Mariah Carey album)")*. The song became Jay-Z's first chart-topper in the U.S., spending two weeks atop the *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* [Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100").[[47]](#cite_note-48) In that same year, Jay-Z released *[Vol. 3... Life and Times of S. Carter](/wiki/Vol._3..._Life_and_Times_of_S._Carter "Vol. 3... Life and Times of S. Carter")*. The album proved successful and sold over 3 million copies.[[44]](#cite_note-riaacertification-45) *Vol. 3*'s most successful single was "[Big Pimpin'](/wiki/Big_Pimpin%27 "Big Pimpin'")", featuring [UGK](/wiki/UGK "UGK").

In 2000, Jay-Z released *[The Dynasty: Roc La Familia](/wiki/The_Dynasty:_Roc_La_Familia "The Dynasty: Roc La Familia")*, which was originally intended to become a compilation album for Roc-A-Fella artists but Def Jam turned into a Jay-Z album.[[48]](#cite_note-49) The album helped to introduce newcomer producers [The Neptunes](/wiki/The_Neptunes "The Neptunes"), [Just Blaze](/wiki/Just_Blaze "Just Blaze"), [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West"), and [Bink](/wiki/Bink_(producer) "Bink (producer)"), which have all gone on to achieve notable success. This is also the first album where Jay-Z utilizes a more soulful sound than his previous albums. *The Dynasty* sold over two million units in the U.S. alone.[[44]](#cite_note-riaacertification-45)

### 2001–2002: Feud with Nas, Prodigy, *The Blueprint* and *The Blueprint2*

In 2001, Jay-Z spoke out against [Prodigy](/wiki/Prodigy_(rapper) "Prodigy (rapper)") after he took an issue with Jay-Z's "[Money, Cash, Hoes](/wiki/Money,_Cash,_Hoes "Money, Cash, Hoes")." Prodigy felt that the lyrics alluded disparagingly to his shared dispute with Mobb Deep against [Tupac Shakur](/wiki/Tupac_Shakur "Tupac Shakur"), [Snoop Dogg](/wiki/Snoop_Dogg "Snoop Dogg"), and [Death Row Records](/wiki/Death_Row_Records "Death Row Records"). He later performed the song "[Takeover](/wiki/Takeover_(Jay-Z_song) "Takeover (Jay-Z song)")", at [Summer Jam](/wiki/Summer_Jam_(festival) "Summer Jam (festival)") 2001, a [diss-track](/wiki/Diss_(music) "Diss (music)") about Nas, Prodigy, and Mobb Deep. At the same concert, Jay-Z brought Michael Jackson up on stage with him.[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51) A line at the end of "Takeover" referred to [Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas"), who criticized him on "We Will Survive".[[51]](#cite_note-52) Nas responded with a diss of his own, and Jay-Z straightaway added a verse to "Takeover" which dissed Nas and would start a feud between the two rappers. The feud had ended by 2005; Jay-Z stated that record producer [Mark Pitts](/wiki/Mark_Pitts "Mark Pitts") had helped them settle their differences.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

On September 11, 2001, Jay-Z released his sixth studio album, *[The Blueprint](/wiki/The_Blueprint "The Blueprint")*, which received a [five-mic review](/wiki/The_Source_(magazine) "The Source (magazine)") from hip-hop magazine *[The Source](/wiki/The_Source_(magazine) "The Source (magazine)").* Written in just two days,[[53]](#cite_note-54) the album sold more than 427,000 copies, debuted at number one on the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200")[[54]](#cite_note-55) and reached double platinum certification by the [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America").[[44]](#cite_note-riaacertification-45) [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem") guest performed and produced its song, "[Renegade](/wiki/Renegade_(Jay-Z_song) "Renegade (Jay-Z song)")". Four tracks were produced by [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West"). *The Blueprint* includes the songs "[Izzo (H.O.V.A.)](/wiki/Izzo_(H.O.V.A.) "Izzo (H.O.V.A.)")", "[Girls, Girls, Girls](/wiki/Girls,_Girls,_Girls_(Jay-Z_song) "Girls, Girls, Girls (Jay-Z song)")", "[Jigga That Nigga](/wiki/Jigga_That_Nigga "Jigga That Nigga")", and "[Song Cry](/wiki/Song_Cry "Song Cry")". As of February 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Jay-Z&action=edit), the album had sold 2.7 million copies worldwide.[[55]](#cite_note-56) In 2019, *The Blueprint* was selected by the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") for preservation in the [National Recording Registry](/wiki/National_Recording_Registry "National Recording Registry") for being "culturally, historically, or aesthetically significant".[[56]](#cite_note-57)

In October 2001, Jay-Z pleaded guilty to aggravated assault for stabbing record producer [Lance Rivera](/wiki/Lance_Rivera "Lance Rivera") at the Kit Kat Klub in New York City in 1999.[[57]](#cite_note-58) Despite Jay-Z's sentence of three years probation for the crime, Rivera later recanted the allegations in 2023.[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)

Jay-Z then collaborated with Chicago singer [R. Kelly](/wiki/R._Kelly "R. Kelly") to release collaborative studio album, *[The Best of Both Worlds](/wiki/The_Best_of_Both_Worlds_(Jay-Z_and_R._Kelly_album) "The Best of Both Worlds (Jay-Z and R. Kelly album)")* in March 2002. In November of that year, Jay-Z released his seventh studio album *[The Blueprint2: The Gift & The Curse](/wiki/The_Blueprint_2:_The_Gift_%26_The_Curse "The Blueprint 2: The Gift & The Curse")*—a double album. The album debuted on the *Billboard* 200 at number one, selling over 3 million units solely in the U.S.[[44]](#cite_note-riaacertification-45) and surpassing *The Blueprint*.[[60]](#cite_note-61) It yielded a single-disc re-issue, *[The Blueprint 2.1](/wiki/The_Blueprint_2:_The_Gift_%26_The_Curse#Blueprint_2.1 "The Blueprint 2: The Gift & The Curse")*, which retained half of the songs from the original. Its original release spawned two hit singles, "[Excuse Me Miss](/wiki/Excuse_Me_Miss "Excuse Me Miss")" and "['03 Bonnie & Clyde](/wiki/%2703_Bonnie_%26_Clyde "'03 Bonnie & Clyde")", which features Jay-Z's then-girlfriend, [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé"), and contained the track "A Dream", featuring [Faith Evans](/wiki/Faith_Evans "Faith Evans") and the late [Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G."); the re-issue spawned the single "[La-La-La](/wiki/La-La-La_(Jay-Z_song) "La-La-La (Jay-Z song)")", which was a sequel to "Excuse Me Miss" and failed to match its commercial success.

### 2003–2005: *The Black Album* and initial retirement

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Jay-Z-02-mika.jpg/250px-Jay-Z-02-mika.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jay-Z-02-mika.jpg)

Jay-Z in 2003

After visiting the south of France,[[61]](#cite_note-retirement-62) Jay-Z announced work on his eighth studio album, *[The Black Album](/wiki/The_Black_Album_(Jay-Z_album) "The Black Album (Jay-Z album)")* at the opening of the 40/40 Club.[[62]](#cite_note-63) He worked with several producers including [Just Blaze](/wiki/Just_Blaze "Just Blaze"), [The Neptunes](/wiki/The_Neptunes "The Neptunes"), [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West"), [Timbaland](/wiki/Timbaland "Timbaland"), [Eminem](/wiki/Eminem "Eminem"), [DJ Quik](/wiki/DJ_Quik "DJ Quik"), [9th Wonder](/wiki/9th_Wonder "9th Wonder"), [The Buchanans](/wiki/The_Buchanans_(production_team) "The Buchanans (production team)"), and [Rick Rubin](/wiki/Rick_Rubin "Rick Rubin"). Notable songs on the album included "[What More Can I Say](/wiki/What_More_Can_I_Say "What More Can I Say")", "[Dirt off Your Shoulder](/wiki/Dirt_off_Your_Shoulder "Dirt off Your Shoulder")", "[Change Clothes](/wiki/Change_Clothes "Change Clothes")", and "[99 Problems](/wiki/99_Problems "99 Problems")". *The Black Album* has sold over 3 million copies in the U.S.[[44]](#cite_note-riaacertification-45)

On November 25, 2003, Jay-Z held a concert—billed as a "retirement party" at [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), which was later the focus of his 2004 documentary, *[Fade to Black](/wiki/Fade_to_Black_(documentary) "Fade to Black (documentary)")*. All proceeds went to charity. Other performers included collaborators [the Roots](/wiki/The_Roots "The Roots") (in the form of his backing band), [Missy Elliott](/wiki/Missy_Elliott "Missy Elliott"), [Memphis Bleek](/wiki/Memphis_Bleek "Memphis Bleek"), [Beanie Siegel](/wiki/Beanie_Siegel "Beanie Siegel"), Freeway, [Mary J. Blige](/wiki/Mary_J._Blige "Mary J. Blige"), Beyoncé, [Twista](/wiki/Twista "Twista"), [Ghostface Killah](/wiki/Ghostface_Killah "Ghostface Killah"), [Foxy Brown](/wiki/Foxy_Brown_(rapper) "Foxy Brown (rapper)"), [Pharrell Williams](/wiki/Pharrell_Williams "Pharrell Williams") and R. Kelly, with appearances by Voletta Wallace and [Afeni Shakur](/wiki/Afeni_Shakur "Afeni Shakur"), the mothers of [the Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G.") and [Tupac Shakur](/wiki/Tupac_Shakur "Tupac Shakur"), respectively.[[63]](#cite_note-retireparty-64) While Jay-Z had attested to a retirement from making new studio albums, various side projects and appearances soon followed. Included in these were a greatest hits record, as well as the release and tour of *[Unfinished Business](/wiki/Unfinished_Business_(Jay-Z_and_R._Kelly_album) "Unfinished Business (Jay-Z and R. Kelly album)")*, the second collaborative album between Jay-Z and R. Kelly.

In 2004, Jay-Z collaborated with rock group [Linkin Park](/wiki/Linkin_Park "Linkin Park"), in which they released their collaborative [remix](/wiki/Remix_album "Remix album") [EP](/wiki/Extended_play "Extended play") *[Collision Course](/wiki/Collision_Course_(EP) "Collision Course (EP)")*, which featured [mashups](/wiki/Mashup_(music) "Mashup (music)") of both artists' songs, as well as a concert DVD. The album's only single, "[Numb/Encore](/wiki/Numb/Encore "Numb/Encore")", went on to win a [Grammy Award for Best Rap/Sung Collaboration](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Rap/Sung_Collaboration "Grammy Award for Best Rap/Sung Collaboration"), and was performed with Linkin Park live at the Grammys, with a special appearance by [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney"), who added verses from the song "[Yesterday](/wiki/Yesterday_(Beatles_song) "Yesterday (Beatles song)")". The EP sold over 1 million copies in the U.S.[[44]](#cite_note-riaacertification-45) Jay-Z was the executive producer of *[The Rising Tied](/wiki/The_Rising_Tied "The Rising Tied")*, the debut album of [Fort Minor](/wiki/Fort_Minor "Fort Minor"), the hip hop [side project](/wiki/Side_project "Side project") of Linkin Park rapper [Mike Shinoda](/wiki/Mike_Shinoda "Mike Shinoda"). Jay-Z also planned to retire in 2004.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

Later in 2004, Jay-Z was named president of Def Jam Records, which led to Jay-Z, Dash and Biggs selling their remaining interests in Roc-A-Fella Records and Jay-Z taking control of both of the companies.[[65]](#cite_note-66) This major industry move was reportedly prompted by disagreements between Jay-Z and Dash as to what direction Roc-A-Fella could undertake.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

### 2005–2007: *Kingdom Come* and *American Gangster*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Jay-Z_concert_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Jay-Z_concert_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jay-Z_concert_(cropped).jpg)

Jay-Z performing in 2006

On October 27, 2005, Jay-Z headlined New York's [Power 105.1](/wiki/Power_105.1 "Power 105.1") annual concert, Powerhouse. The concert's title, "I Declare War," led to intense speculation in the preceding weeks on whom exactly Jay-Z would declare war. As he had previously "declared war" on other artists taking lyrical shots at him at other events, many believed that the concert's title represented an all-out assault by Jay-Z upon his rivals.[[67]](#cite_note-68)[[68]](#cite_note-69) The theme of the concert was Jay-Z's position as president and CEO of [Def Jam](/wiki/Def_Jam_Recordings "Def Jam Recordings"), complete with an on-stage mock-up of the [Oval Office](/wiki/Oval_Office "Oval Office"). Many artists made appearances such as the old roster of [Roc-A-Fella](/wiki/Roc-A-Fella "Roc-A-Fella") records artists, as well as [Ne-Yo](/wiki/Ne-Yo "Ne-Yo"), [Teairra Marí](/wiki/Teairra_Mar%C3%AD "Teairra Marí"), [T.I.](/wiki/T.I. "T.I."), [Young Jeezy](/wiki/Young_Jeezy "Young Jeezy"), [Akon](/wiki/Akon "Akon"), [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West"), [Paul Wall](/wiki/Paul_Wall "Paul Wall"), [the LOX](/wiki/The_LOX "The LOX"), and [Sean Combs](/wiki/Sean_Combs "Sean Combs").[[69]](#cite_note-allhiphop-70)

At the conclusion of the concert, Jay-Z put many arguments to rest to the surprise of hip hop fans. The most significant development in this show was closure to the infamous hip hop rivalry between Jay-Z and [Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas"). The two former rivals shook hands and shared the stage together to perform Jay-Z's "Dead Presidents" blended with Nas's song "The World is Yours".[[70]](#cite_note-mtvnews2-71)

Jay-Z returned with his comeback album on November 21, 2006, titled *[Kingdom Come](/wiki/Kingdom_Come_(Jay-Z_album) "Kingdom Come (Jay-Z album)")*.[[71]](#cite_note-72) Jay-Z's comeback single, "[Show Me What You Got](/wiki/Show_Me_What_You_Got "Show Me What You Got")", was leaked on the Internet in early October 2006. The album, which was scheduled for release later on that month, received heavy air-play after its leak, causing the [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI") to step in and investigate.[[72]](#cite_note-73) Jay-Z worked with video director [Hype Williams](/wiki/Hype_Williams "Hype Williams"), and the single's video was directed by [F. Gary Gray](/wiki/F._Gary_Gray "F. Gary Gray"). The album features production from [Just Blaze](/wiki/Just_Blaze "Just Blaze"), [Pharrell](/wiki/Pharrell "Pharrell"), [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West"), [Dr. Dre](/wiki/Dr._Dre "Dr. Dre") and [Coldplay](/wiki/Coldplay "Coldplay")'s [Chris Martin](/wiki/Chris_Martin "Chris Martin") (single entitled "[Beach Chair](/wiki/Kingdom_Come_(Jay-Z_album) "Kingdom Come (Jay-Z album)")").[[73]](#cite_note-sfgate-74)[[74]](#cite_note-mtvnews-75) The first week saw 680,000 sales of the CD, enough to be his "biggest sales week ever" as *Billboard* reported.[[75]](#cite_note-billboardkingdom-76) This album has sold 2 million copies in the U.S. and is certified double platinum.[[44]](#cite_note-riaacertification-45)[[76]](#cite_note-77)

Jay-Z released his tenth album entitled *[American Gangster](/wiki/American_Gangster_(album) "American Gangster (album)")* on November 6, 2007. After viewing the Ridley Scott [film of the same name](/wiki/American_Gangster_(film) "American Gangster (film)"), Jay-Z was heavily inspired to create a new "concept" album that depicts his experiences as a street-hustler.[[77]](#cite_note-newyorktimes-78)
The album is not the film's official soundtrack, although it was distributed by [Def Jam](/wiki/Def_Jam_Recordings "Def Jam Recordings").[[78]](#cite_note-hiphopdx-79)
Jay-Z's *American Gangster* depicts his life in correlation to the movie *American Gangster*. At the start of the album's first single, "[Blue Magic](/wiki/Blue_Magic_(song) "Blue Magic (song)")", Jay-Z offers a dealer's manifesto while making references to political figures of the late 1980s with the lyric: "Blame [Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") for making me to into a monster, blame [Oliver North](/wiki/Oliver_North "Oliver North") and [Iran-Contra](/wiki/Iran-Contra "Iran-Contra"), I ran contraband that they sponsored, before this rhymin' stuff we was in concert."[[79]](#cite_note-80) Also notable about the "Blue Magic" music video was Jay-Z flashing €500 notes; [Harvard Business School](/wiki/Harvard_Business_School "Harvard Business School") professor [Rawi Abdelal](/wiki/Rawi_Abdelal "Rawi Abdelal") called this a "turning point in American pop culture's response to globalization." The album has sold 1 million copies in the U.S.[[44]](#cite_note-riaacertification-45) On January 1, 2008, Jay-Z resigned as president of Def Jam.[[80]](#cite_note-81)

### 2008–2011: *The Blueprint 3* and *Watch the Throne*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Jay-Z_3.jpg/250px-Jay-Z_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jay-Z_3.jpg)

Jay-Z performing in 2008

In winter 2008, it was announced that Jay-Z would become the first major hip hop artist to headline Britain's [Glastonbury Festival](/wiki/Glastonbury_Festival_2008 "Glastonbury Festival 2008").[[81]](#cite_note-glastonbury-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83) Tickets sold out quickly. Former headliner [Noel Gallagher](/wiki/Noel_Gallagher "Noel Gallagher") of [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)") condemned the festival organizers for choosing Jay-Z to headline a traditionally guitar-driven festival. "I'm sorry, but Jay-Z?" Gallagher asked, swearing. "No chance. Glastonbury has a tradition of, kind of, guitar music, do you know what I mean? And even when they throw the odd curve balls in on a Sunday night—you go, 'Kylie Minogue? I don't know about that', do you know what I mean?—but I'm not having hip hop at Glastonbury, no way, no, no. It's wrong."[[83]](#cite_note-84) As controversy mounted, Jay-Z replied, "We don't play guitars, Noel, but hip hop has put in its work like any other form of music. This headline show is just a natural progression. Rap music is still evolving. We have to respect each other's genre of music and move forward."[[84]](#cite_note-85) Jay-Z opened his Glastonbury set with a tongue-in-cheek cover of Oasis's iconic song "[Wonderwall](/wiki/Wonderwall_(song) "Wonderwall (song)")",[[85]](#cite_note-glastonbury3-86) and went on to deliver a performance heralded as a successful response to pre-festival criticism.[[86]](#cite_note-87) The guitar used in this performance was later shown at the Book of HOV exhibit in the Brooklyn Library.[[87]](#cite_note-88)[[88]](#cite_note-89)[[89]](#cite_note-90)

He also headlined many other summer festivals in 2008, including [Roskilde Festival](/wiki/Roskilde_Festival "Roskilde Festival") in Denmark,[[90]](#cite_note-91) [Hove Festival](/wiki/Hove_Festival "Hove Festival") in Norway[[91]](#cite_note-92) and [O2 Wireless Festival](/wiki/O2_Wireless_Festival "O2 Wireless Festival") in London.[[92]](#cite_note-93) During Kanye West's concert of August 6, 2008, at Madison Square Garden, Jay-Z came out to perform a new song and he and West proclaimed that it was to be on *The Blueprint 3*.[[93]](#cite_note-94) On May 21, 2009, Jay-Z announced he would be parting ways with Def Jam, and had struck a multi-million-dollar deal to sign with [Live Nation](/wiki/Live_Nation "Live Nation"), with whom he would start his [Roc Nation](/wiki/Roc_Nation "Roc Nation") imprint which would serve as a record label, talent/management agency, and music publishing company[[94]](#cite_note-hitsdailydouble-95) and also partnered with production team [Stargate](/wiki/Stargate_(production_team) "Stargate (production team)") to start a record label called [StarRoc](/wiki/StarRoc "StarRoc").[[95]](#cite_note-96)[[96]](#cite_note-97) Jay-Z's 11th studio album *[The Blueprint 3](/wiki/The_Blueprint_3 "The Blueprint 3")* was originally to be released on September 11, 2009,[[97]](#cite_note-rollingstonerocnation-98) but was instead released in North America on September 8, 2009, due to increasing anticipation.[[98]](#cite_note-rocnationblueprint3-99) Its international release followed on September 14.[[99]](#cite_note-100) It is his 11th album to reach No. 1 on the Billboard 200[[100]](#cite_note-101) and surpassed Elvis Presley's previous record.[[101]](#cite_note-accesshollywood-102) On October 9, 2009, Jay-Z kicked off his tour for *The Blueprint 3*, during which he supported his new album in North America. In a *Shave Magazine* review of his performance at [Rexall Place](/wiki/Rexall_Place "Rexall Place") in [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton "Edmonton"), Jake Tomlinson expressed that "It was the type of smooth performance you would expect from the hip-hop superstar." The review gave this performance 4 stars. His North American tour continued until November 22, 2009.[[102]](#cite_note-shavemagazine-103) At his concert on November 8, 2009, at [UCLA](/wiki/UCLA "UCLA")'s [Pauley Pavilion](/wiki/Pauley_Pavilion "Pauley Pavilion"), [Rihanna](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna") joined him on stage and performed "Hard" for the first time, then performed "Run This Town" with Jay-Z.[[103]](#cite_note-104) Among his success, Jay-Z has ventured into producing Broadway shows. Along with Jada Pinkett Smith and Will Smith, Jay-Z helped produce the play *Fela!*, a musical celebrating the work of the late Nigerian star [Fela Kuti](/wiki/Fela_Kuti "Fela Kuti").[[104]](#cite_note-fela-105) Jay-Z said he was inspired by the power of Kuti's work and his life story, which resulted in his interest to help produce the musical.[[104]](#cite_note-fela-105) *Fela!* is a story about an African pioneer and political activist who made his first moves on the scene during the 1970s.[[104]](#cite_note-fela-105)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Jay-zlivein2010.jpg/250px-Jay-zlivein2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jay-zlivein2010.jpg)

Jay-Z performing at the [Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival](/wiki/Coachella_Valley_Music_and_Arts_Festival "Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival") in April 2010

On January 23, 2010, Jay-Z released a track, "[Stranded (Haiti Mon Amour)](/wiki/Stranded_(Haiti_Mon_Amour) "Stranded (Haiti Mon Amour)")", with Rihanna, and [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2")'s [Bono](/wiki/Bono "Bono") and [The Edge](/wiki/The_Edge "The Edge"), and performed it at the [Hope For Haiti Now](/wiki/Hope_For_Haiti_Now "Hope For Haiti Now") telethon.[[105]](#cite_note-106) In June 2010, Eminem and Jay-Z announced they would perform together in a pair of concerts in Detroit and New York. The event was dubbed [The Home & Home Tour](/wiki/The_Home_%26_Home_Tour "The Home & Home Tour"). The first two concerts rapidly sold out, prompting the scheduling of an additional show at each venue.[[106]](#cite_note-107) Jay-Z was the supporting act for U2 on the Australian and New Zealand leg of their [U2 360° Tour](/wiki/U2_360%C2%B0_Tour "U2 360° Tour"), beginning in [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"), New Zealand, in November 2010, followed by Melbourne, Sydney, [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") and [Perth](/wiki/Perth "Perth") in December.[[107]](#cite_note-108)

Jay-Z would later appear with Kanye West on *[Watch the Throne](/wiki/Watch_the_Throne "Watch the Throne")*, a full-length LP with origins as a five-track EP. Recording sessions for the album took place at various recording locations and began in November 2010. The first single released for the project was "[H•A•M](/wiki/H%E2%80%A2A%E2%80%A2M "H•A•M")". The track was co-produced by [Lex Luger](/wiki/Lex_Luger_(producer) "Lex Luger (producer)") and West himself. The track ended up being on the deluxe edition of the album. The follow-up to that was the second single "[Otis](/wiki/Otis_(song) "Otis (song)")", which premiered on [Funkmaster Flex](/wiki/Funkmaster_Flex "Funkmaster Flex")'s [Hot 97](/wiki/Hot_97 "Hot 97") radio show, and was later released to the [iTunes Store](/wiki/ITunes_Store "ITunes Store") eleven days later. The song's existence, along with several other tracks from the album, was confirmed during a listening session hosted by Jay-Z.[[108]](#cite_note-109) The album was first released on the iTunes Store, five days prior to its being released in physical format, a strategy Jay-Z later said was used to block an [internet leak](/wiki/Music_leak "Music leak"). It debuted at No. 1 on the iTunes Store in 23 countries. It also broke [Coldplay](/wiki/Coldplay "Coldplay")'s record for most albums sold in one week on the online retailer, selling 290,000 copies on iTunes alone. It held that record until [Lil Wayne](/wiki/Lil_Wayne "Lil Wayne")'s *[Tha Carter IV](/wiki/Tha_Carter_IV "Tha Carter IV")* was released twenty-one days later, selling 10,000 copies more. It debuted on the U.S. [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") chart at No. 1, selling 436,000 copies in its first week. The album received generally positive reviews. Jay-Z and West later gave a surprise performance of "Otis" at the [2011 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2011_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2011 MTV Video Music Awards").

### 2012–2016: *Magna Carta Holy Grail* and other ventures

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Jay-Z_Beyonce.jpg/250px-Jay-Z_Beyonce.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jay-Z_Beyonce.jpg)

Jay-Z and his wife [Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9 "Beyoncé") after his performance of "[Tom Ford](/wiki/Tom_Ford_(song) "Tom Ford (song)")" on [The Mrs. Carter Show World Tour](/wiki/The_Mrs._Carter_Show_World_Tour "The Mrs. Carter Show World Tour") in 2013

In May 2012, Jay-Z and former [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") mayor [Michael A. Nutter](/wiki/Michael_A._Nutter "Michael A. Nutter") announced Jay-Z as the curator and the headliner for the first annual "Budweiser Made in America" festival at [Fairmount Park](/wiki/Fairmount_Park "Fairmount Park") in Philadelphia on September 1 and 2, 2012. The performance was produced by [Live Nation](/wiki/Live_Nation_Entertainment "Live Nation Entertainment") and assembled an eclectic lineup of "[rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"), [hip hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music"), [R&B](/wiki/Contemporary_R%26B "Contemporary R&B"), [Latin music](/wiki/Latin_music_(genre) "Latin music (genre)") and dance" musicians.[[109]](#cite_note-110) Jay-Z and [Rihanna](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna") were the two main headlining acts for [BBC Radio 1](/wiki/BBC_Radio_1 "BBC Radio 1")'s [2012 Hackney Weekend](/wiki/BBC_Radio_1%27s_Big_Weekend "BBC Radio 1's Big Weekend") music festival on June 23 to 24.[[110]](#cite_note-111) Jay-Z opened his set with an appearance from Rihanna, they performed "Run this Town". On September 6, "[Clique](/wiki/Clique_(Kanye_West_Song) "Clique (Kanye West Song)")" was released, a single from the album "[Cruel Summer](/wiki/Cruel_Summer_(GOOD_Music_album) "Cruel Summer (GOOD Music album)")", by [GOOD Music](/wiki/GOOD_Music "GOOD Music"). [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West") and [Big Sean](/wiki/Big_Sean "Big Sean") starred alongside Jay-Z on the track. Jay-Z took the subway to his sold-out show at The Barclays Center on October 6, 2012.[[111]](#cite_note-112) In December 2012, Coldplay performed with Jay-Z at the [Barclays Center](/wiki/Barclays_Center "Barclays Center") in Brooklyn.[[112]](#cite_note-113)

On September 23, 2010, [Q-Tip](/wiki/Q-Tip_(rapper) "Q-Tip (rapper)") confirmed working on Jay-Z's follow-up album to *The Blueprint 3*, saying the album was to hit stores by spring 2011.[[113]](#cite_note-114) In May 2012 it was reported that Jay-Z would work on new music with [Roc Nation](/wiki/Roc_Nation "Roc Nation") producer [Jahlil Beats](/wiki/Jahlil_Beats "Jahlil Beats"). Beats told *[XXL](/wiki/XXL_(magazine) "XXL (magazine)")* magazine: "Me and Jay-Z been going back and forth. He picked a couple of my joints that he's working on. I don't even wanna say too much about Jay, but we definitely working on some stuff. I haven't even sent him a bunch of beats. I sent him my favorite stuff. He hit me right back like, 'Yo, I'ma go in on this,' or, 'I like this.'"[[114]](#cite_note-115) The album was named one of the most anticipated albums of 2013 by *[Complex Magazine](/wiki/Complex_Magazine "Complex Magazine")*, [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV"), and *XXL*. The album was produced by [Jahlil Beats](/wiki/Jahlil_Beats "Jahlil Beats"), [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West"), [Rick Rubin](/wiki/Rick_Rubin "Rick Rubin"), [Swizz Beatz](/wiki/Swizz_Beatz "Swizz Beatz"), [Timbaland](/wiki/Timbaland "Timbaland"), and [Pharrell Williams](/wiki/Pharrell_Williams "Pharrell Williams").[[115]](#cite_note-116)[[116]](#cite_note-117) Jay-Z also co-produced and performed on [Justin Timberlake](/wiki/Justin_Timberlake "Justin Timberlake")'s comeback single "[Suit & Tie](/wiki/Suit_%26_Tie "Suit & Tie")" from his third studio album *[The 20/20 Experience](/wiki/The_20/20_Experience "The 20/20 Experience")*, the song itself was produced by both Jay-Z and Timberlake's mutual friend, Timbaland.[[117]](#cite_note-118) In July 2013, Timberlake and Jay-Z embarked on the co-headlining [Legends of the Summer Stadium Tour](/wiki/Legends_of_the_Summer_Stadium_Tour "Legends of the Summer Stadium Tour").[[118]](#cite_note-119)

During the fifth game of the [2013 NBA Finals](/wiki/2013_NBA_Finals "2013 NBA Finals"), Carter announced his twelfth studio album, *[Magna Carta Holy Grail](/wiki/Magna_Carta_Holy_Grail "Magna Carta Holy Grail")*, and was released on July 4, 2013. Not long after, Jay-Z confirmed that the hyphen in his stage name would be left out and officially stylized in all capital letters.[[119]](#cite_note-120) *Magna Carta Holy Grail* debuted at number one on the *Billboard* 200 and sold 528,000 copies in its first week, which bypassed its predicted debut in the range of 350,000 to 400,000.[[120]](#cite_note-121)
In December 2013, it was announced that Jay-Z had received nine nominations at the [2014 Grammy Awards](/wiki/2014_Grammy_Awards "2014 Grammy Awards"), more than any other artist.[[121]](#cite_note-122) Jay-Z appeared on his wife Beyoncé's self-titled fifth studio album, *[Beyoncé](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9_(album) "Beyoncé (album)")*, with a feature on the song "[Drunk in Love](/wiki/Drunk_in_Love "Drunk in Love")". They performed this song together at the [56th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/56th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "56th Annual Grammy Awards") opening.[[122]](#cite_note-123) The song and its accompanying album would later win three Grammy Awards including [Best R&B Performance](/wiki/Best_R%26B_Performance "Best R&B Performance") at the [2015 ceremony](/wiki/57th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "57th Annual Grammy Awards"). In 2016, he won a lawsuit for the song "Made in America" with Kanye West featuring [Frank Ocean](/wiki/Frank_Ocean "Frank Ocean") against Joel McDonald.[[123]](#cite_note-124)

### 2017–present: *4:44*, *Everything Is Love* and Book of HOV exhibit

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Everything_Is_Love_Album_Release_-_OTR_II_Tour.jpg/250px-Everything_Is_Love_Album_Release_-_OTR_II_Tour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Everything_Is_Love_Album_Release_-_OTR_II_Tour.jpg)

Jay-Z announcing the *[Everything Is Love](/wiki/Everything_Is_Love "Everything Is Love")* album in London in 2018

In early June 2017, posters were displayed in New York City and Los Angeles, and banner ads were placed on the Internet promoting a [Tidal](/wiki/Tidal_(service) "Tidal (service)")-related project titled *[4:44](/wiki/4:44 "4:44")*.[[124]](#cite_note-Jay_Z_Details_New_Album_'4:44'-125) A teaser ad was aired during the [NBA Finals](/wiki/2017_NBA_Finals "2017 NBA Finals") on June 7 featuring actors [Mahershala Ali](/wiki/Mahershala_Ali "Mahershala Ali"), [Lupita Nyong'o](/wiki/Lupita_Nyong%27o "Lupita Nyong'o") and [Danny Glover](/wiki/Danny_Glover "Danny Glover") in a one-minute video, ending in "4:44 – 6.30.17, Exclusively on Tidal". On June 18, the project was confirmed to be a new Jay-Z album, and a clip featuring a song titled "Adnis" was posted on Sprint's YouTube page.[[124]](#cite_note-Jay_Z_Details_New_Album_'4:44'-125) *4:44* was released through [Roc Nation](/wiki/Roc_Nation "Roc Nation") and [Universal Music Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group"), as an exclusive to [Sprint](/wiki/Sprint_Corporation "Sprint Corporation") and [Tidal](/wiki/Tidal_(service) "Tidal (service)") customers.[[125]](#cite_note-126) The album is the first in a planned series of music exclusives from the Sprint–Tidal partnership. For a short time, on July 2, the album was made available for free digital download in Tidal's site. A physical edition was released on July 7, including three additional tracks. On the same day, the album was made available to other streaming platforms, such as [Apple Music](/wiki/Apple_Music "Apple Music"), [Google Play Music](/wiki/Google_Play_Music "Google Play Music") and [Amazon Music](/wiki/Amazon_Music "Amazon Music").

The album received widespread acclaim from critics, who praised its emotional and personal content. On July 5, the album was certified [Platinum](/wiki/RIAA_certification "RIAA certification") by the [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America") (RIAA), in recognition of one million copies purchased by Sprint and offered to consumers as free downloads.[[126]](#cite_note-platinum-127) It debuted at number one on the U.S. [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200"), making it Jay-Z's 13th consecutive studio album to top the chart. The album spawned two singles, the title track "4:44" and "Bam", as well as several music videos, directed by a variety of high-profile collaborators. The album received a [Grammy Award](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award") nomination for [Album of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Album_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Album of the Year"), while the title track was nominated for [Song of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Song_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Song of the Year") and "[The Story of O.J.](/wiki/The_Story_of_O.J. "The Story of O.J.")" was nominated for [Record of the Year](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Record_of_the_Year "Grammy Award for Record of the Year") at the [60th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/60th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "60th Annual Grammy Awards").[[127]](#cite_note-128) Two years later, he became hip-hop's first artist to achieve billionaire status.[[128]](#cite_note-129)

On June 6, 2018, Jay-Z and Beyoncé kicked-off the [On the Run II Tour](/wiki/On_the_Run_II_Tour "On the Run II Tour") in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff"), United Kingdom. Ten days later, at their final London performance, the pair unveiled *[Everything Is Love](/wiki/Everything_Is_Love "Everything Is Love")*, their much-awaited joint studio album, credited under the name *The Carters*. The pair also released the video for the album's lead single, "[Apeshit](/wiki/Apeshit "Apeshit")", on Beyoncé's official [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") channel.[[129]](#cite_note-130)[[130]](#cite_note-131) The song won two awards from eight nominations at the [2018 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2018_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2018 MTV Video Music Awards").[[131]](#cite_note-132)

In 2021, Jay-Z was inducted into the [Rock and Roll Hall of Fame](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Hall_of_Fame "Rock and Roll Hall of Fame") along with fellow rapper [LL Cool J](/wiki/LL_Cool_J "LL Cool J").[[132]](#cite_note-133) He also appeared on the song "[Jail](/wiki/Jail_(Kanye_West_song) "Jail (Kanye West song)")" on Kanye West's 2021 album *[Donda](/wiki/Donda "Donda")*,[[133]](#cite_note-134) which went on to win [Grammy Award for Best Rap Song](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Rap_Song "Grammy Award for Best Rap Song") at the [64th Annual Grammy Awards](/wiki/64th_Annual_Grammy_Awards "64th Annual Grammy Awards")[[134]](#cite_note-135) and on the song "Love All" from [Drake](/wiki/Drake_(musician) "Drake (musician)")'s 2021 album *[Certified Lover Boy](/wiki/Certified_Lover_Boy "Certified Lover Boy")*.[[135]](#cite_note-136) In 2022, his first feature was a collaboration with fellow rapper [Pusha T](/wiki/Pusha_T "Pusha T"), "[Neck & Wrist](/wiki/Neck_%26_Wrist "Neck & Wrist")" featuring [Pharrell Williams](/wiki/Pharrell_Williams "Pharrell Williams") from Pusha T's album, *[It's Almost Dry](/wiki/It%27s_Almost_Dry "It's Almost Dry")*.[[136]](#cite_note-137) His feature was succeeded by a four-minute guest appearance on [DJ Khaled](/wiki/DJ_Khaled "DJ Khaled")'s [album](/wiki/God_Did "God Did") title track, "[God Did](/wiki/God_Did_(song) "God Did (song)")".[[137]](#cite_note-138)[[138]](#cite_note-139) He also was awarded the [Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Variety Special (Live)](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Variety_Special_(Live) "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Variety Special (Live)"), as a producer for the [Super Bowl LVI halftime show](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVI_halftime_show "Super Bowl LVI halftime show").[[139]](#cite_note-140) In 2023, the Brooklyn Library featured the Book of HOV exhibit. The showcase included artifacts commemorating Jay-Z's career.[[140]](#cite_note-141) The next year, the exhibit was turned into a book, *The Book of HOV: A Tribute to Jay-Z*. Similar to the exhibit, the physical book recounted Jay-Z's life and career, including images, interviews, and professional insights.[[141]](#cite_note-142)

In 2026, Jay-Z released "Dead Presidents" on streaming services and a limited-edition vinyl, CD and cassette tape version on his website, to celebrate the 30th anniversary of his debut studio album *Reasonable Doubt*. He also added the umlaut to his stage name, which was a callback to his artwork of the album.[[142]](#cite_note-143)[[143]](#cite_note-144)

## Musical style

### Influences

Jay-Z says his earliest exposure to music was through his parents' record collection, which was mostly of [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") artists such as [Marvin Gaye](/wiki/Marvin_Gaye "Marvin Gaye") and [Donny Hathaway](/wiki/Donny_Hathaway "Donny Hathaway"). He says "I grew up around music, listening to all types of people... I'm into music that has soul in it, whether it be rap, R&B, pop music, whatever. As long as I can feel their soul through the wax, that's what I really listen to."[[144]](#cite_note-MTV_News-145) He often uses excerpts from these artists as [samples](/wiki/Sampling_(music) "Sampling (music)") in his work, particularly in the Kanye West productions included on *The Blueprint.*[[144]](#cite_note-MTV_News-145)

### Rapping technique

Known for his lyrical prowess which often utilises [double entendres](/wiki/Double_entendre "Double entendre"), witty [wordplays](/wiki/Word_play "Word play"), and mixes messages with [braggadocio](/wiki/Braggadocio_(rap) "Braggadocio (rap)"), his music often describes his rise from poverty to vast wealth.[[145]](#cite_note-146)[[146]](#cite_note-147)[[147]](#cite_note-148)[[148]](#cite_note-149)[[149]](#cite_note-150)

[Royce da 5'9"](/wiki/Royce_da_5%279%22 "Royce da 5'9\"") and [Fredro Starr](/wiki/Fredro_Starr "Fredro Starr") of [Onyx](/wiki/Onyx_(hip_hop_group) "Onyx (hip hop group)") both describe Jay-Z's emphasis on [flow](/wiki/Flow_(music) "Flow (music)") in the book *[How to Rap](/wiki/How_to_Rap "How to Rap")*—Starr says that Jay-Z is "a master of the flow—he can flow fast, he can flow slow".[[150]](#cite_note-EdwardsRap65-151) The book describes how Jay-Z uses 'rests' to provide structure to a verse[[151]](#cite_note-EdwardsRap129-152) and how he uses 'partial linking' to add more [rhymes](/wiki/Rhyme "Rhyme") to a verse.[[152]](#cite_note-EdwardsRap91-153) Jay-Z's early style is described by [*Vibe*](/wiki/Vibe_(magazine) "Vibe (magazine)") as "a distinctly [Das EFX](/wiki/Das_EFX "Das EFX")-type, stiggety style" on his 12" single "Can't Get With That",[[153]](#cite_note-154) referring to the fast rhythms and vocal delivery of the group Das EFX. He is also known to write lyrics in his head, as described by [Pusha T](/wiki/Pusha_T "Pusha T") of [Clipse](/wiki/Clipse "Clipse") in *How to Rap*,[[154]](#cite_note-EdwardsRap144-155) a style popular with many [MCs](/wiki/MC "MC") such as [The Notorious B.I.G.](/wiki/The_Notorious_B.I.G. "The Notorious B.I.G."),[[154]](#cite_note-EdwardsRap144-155) [Everlast](/wiki/Everlast_(musician) "Everlast (musician)"),[[155]](#cite_note-EdwardsRap144-145-156) [Bobby Creekwater](/wiki/Bobby_Creekwater "Bobby Creekwater")[[156]](#cite_note-EdwardsRap145-157) and [Guerilla Black](/wiki/Guerilla_Black "Guerilla Black").[[156]](#cite_note-EdwardsRap145-157) [Shock G](/wiki/Shock_G "Shock G") of [Digital Underground](/wiki/Digital_Underground "Digital Underground") describes Jay-Z's live performance style, saying he "rarely breaks a sweat, and instead uses smoothness and clever wordplay to keep the audience interested and entertained".[[157]](#cite_note-EdwardsRap292-158) Jay-Z's fast rapping technique, also known as the ["triplet time"](/wiki/Rapping#Rhythm "Rapping"), was developed during his early years of creating music with former mentor [Jaz-O](/wiki/Jaz-O "Jaz-O").[[158]](#cite_note-159)[[159]](#cite_note-160)

## Business career

[Dean Baquet](/wiki/Dean_Baquet "Dean Baquet")'s 2017 interview with Jay Z for *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*

Jay-Z has also established himself as a successful entrepreneur with a business empire spanning a variety of industries from clothing lines, beverages, real estate, sport teams, and record labels.[[160]](#cite_note-161) In an interview, he stated that "my brands are an extension of me. They're close to me. It's not like running [GM](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors"), where there's no emotional attachment."[[161]](#cite_note-162)

### Rocawear

Jay-Z and [Damon Dash](/wiki/Damon_Dash "Damon Dash") are the founders of the urban clothing brand [Rocawear](/wiki/Rocawear "Rocawear").[[162]](#cite_note-NYTimes1-163) Rocawear has clothing lines and accessories for men, women and children. The line was taken over by Jay-Z in early 2006 following a falling out with Dash. In March 2007, Jay-Z sold the rights to the Rocawear brand to [Iconix Brand Group](/wiki/Iconix_Brand_Group "Iconix Brand Group") for $204 million. He retains his stake in the company and continues to oversee the marketing, licensing and product development.[[162]](#cite_note-NYTimes1-163)[[163]](#cite_note-164)

### Reebok

Jay-Z became the first rapper to endorse [Reebok](/wiki/Reebok "Reebok")'s footwear, and signed a three-year endorsement deal with them.[[164]](#cite_note-:1-165) He later appeared in a 2003 Reebok collection advertising his S. Carter Collection.[[165]](#cite_note-166) Later that year, he and [frenemy](/wiki/Frenemy "Frenemy") [50 Cent](/wiki/50_Cent "50 Cent") appeared in a commercial to promote their S. Carter and [G-Unit](/wiki/G-Unit_Clothing_Company "G-Unit Clothing Company") footwears for the company, with a Just Blaze-produced song made for it.[[166]](#cite_note-167)

In 2006, Jay-Z's deal with Reebok expired with no renewal.[[164]](#cite_note-:1-165)

### Alcoholic beverages

In 2014, Jay-Z invested $200 million in [Armand de Brignac](/wiki/Armand_de_Brignac "Armand de Brignac") champagne—owned at the time by Sovereign Brands, a New York–based wine and spirits company—for a 100 percent stake, making it the second alcoholic product acquisition in his financial investment portfolio.[[167]](#cite_note-168)[[168]](#cite_note-169) The brand is known for its popularity with high-profile artists as being the gold bottles often referred to in media.[[169]](#cite_note-170)[[170]](#cite_note-171)[[171]](#cite_note-172)[[172]](#cite_note-173)[[173]](#cite_note-174) His ties to the company date back to 2006, and he received millions of dollars per year for his association with Armand de Brignac before he bought the entire company.[[174]](#cite_note-175)

Jay-Z serves as co-brand director for Budweiser Select and collaborates with the company on strategic marketing programs and creative ad development. He provides direction on brand programs and ads that appear on TV, radio, print, and high-profile events.[[175]](#cite_note-176)

### Technology

In March 2015, Jay-Z completed the $56 million acquisition of [Aspiro](/wiki/Aspiro "Aspiro"), a Norwegian media technology company that operates the subscription-based music streaming service [Tidal](/wiki/Tidal_(service) "Tidal (service)"), which has been in operation since October 2014.[[176]](#cite_note-177) The music service was acquired through his company [Project Panther Bidco Ltd.](/wiki/Project_Panther_Bidco "Project Panther Bidco") (an entity indirectly owned by Jay-Z's S. Carter Enterprises a company holding interests in leading international music, media and entertainment companies).[[177]](#cite_note-178)[[178]](#cite_note-179) The music service combines audio and music videos with curated editorial. The main idea of the service is to bring major revenue streams back to the music artists themselves as the idea of an artist-owned streaming platform was stated as to "restore the value to music by launching a service owned by artists." Jay-Z currently[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] is a major shareholder in the company.[[179]](#cite_note-180)[[180]](#cite_note-181)

In July 2015, Carter made a significant investment to [JetSmarter](/wiki/JetSmarter "JetSmarter"), an app helping people book private executive plane flights. The app was built by Sergey Petrossov.[[181]](#cite_note-182)

### Music industry

From 2005 to 2007, Jay-Z was inaugurated as the president of Def Jam Recordings. Under Jay-Z's leadership, Def Jam launched the successful careers of contemporary R&B singers [Rihanna](/wiki/Rihanna "Rihanna") and [Ne-Yo](/wiki/Ne-Yo "Ne-Yo"). At the end of 2007, after he released [*American Gangster*](/wiki/American_Gangster_(album) "American Gangster (album)"), Jay-Z decided not to renew his contract as the president and CEO of Def Jam. Shortly after, he started his [Live Nation](/wiki/Live_Nation_Entertainment "Live Nation Entertainment") venture, [Roc Nation](/wiki/Roc_Nation "Roc Nation").[[182]](#cite_note-183)

In April 2011, it was reported that Jay-Z had outbid executives at [Universal Music](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group") and [Jive Records](/wiki/Jive_Records "Jive Records") to acquire independent record label [Block Starz Music](/wiki/Block_Starz_Music "Block Starz Music").[[183]](#cite_note-184)[[184]](#cite_note-185)

### Sporting business

From 2003 to 2013, Jay-Z was a part-owner of the [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets") [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") team, having paid a reported $1 million for his share, which declined in value to $350,000 in April 2013, based on *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* magazine's valuation of the team.[[185]](#cite_note-186) He encouraged the team's relocation to [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn")'s [Barclays Center](/wiki/Barclays_Center "Barclays Center") (from [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey")) in the [2012–2013 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_NBA_season "2012–13 NBA season"), at which point the team took on the Brooklyn Nets moniker.[[186]](#cite_note-187)[[187]](#cite_note-188)

On April 18, 2013, Jay-Z officially announced through his Life + Times website in a press release that he would be relinquishing his ownership in the Brooklyn Nets.[[188]](#cite_note-189) The shares were eventually sold to singer, rapper, actor and entrepreneur [Will Pan](/wiki/Will_Pan "Will Pan"), making Pan the first American of Taiwanese descent to own a U.S. professional sports franchise.[[189]](#cite_note-:0-190)[[190]](#cite_note-:2-191) Jay-Z's cited Pan's athletic background (he was a team captain of his high school basketball team and played in college), his multitude of musical styles, his influence in the Taiwanese American community, and his business acumen and portfolio (including being the founder and chief executive of software company Camigo Media and a co-founder of streetwear boutique N.P.C [New Project Center]), as reasons why his bid was successful.[[189]](#cite_note-:0-190)[[190]](#cite_note-:2-191)

In September 2013, his stake in [Barclays Center](/wiki/Barclays_Center "Barclays Center") was sold for $1.5 million.[[191]](#cite_note-192)

On April 2, 2013, [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") reported Jay-Z's plans to launch his own sports agency, [Roc Nation Sports](/wiki/Roc_Nation_Sports "Roc Nation Sports"), with a focus on the representation of various professional athletes. The [sport management](/wiki/Sport_management "Sport management") group is a partnership with [Creative Artists Agency](/wiki/Creative_Artists_Agency "Creative Artists Agency").[[192]](#cite_note-ESPNgo-193) In conjunction with the agency's launch, [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees")'s second baseman [Robinson Canó](/wiki/Robinson_Can%C3%B3 "Robinson Canó") left agent [Scott Boras](/wiki/Scott_Boras "Scott Boras") to sign with the company. ESPN also mentioned that Jay-Z himself was planning to be a certified sports agent, first in baseball and eventually in basketball and football. In order to represent clients in basketball, he would have to give up his small share of the [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets").[[192]](#cite_note-ESPNgo-193)[[193]](#cite_note-194)

In October 2005, he was reported in English media as considering buying a stake of [Arsenal F.C.](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C."), an English soccer team.[[194]](#cite_note-195) Through his conglomerate company Gain Global Investments Network LLC, he had an interest estimated between 2 and 7% in the Aqueduct Entertainment Group (AEG) consortium, which in January 2010 was awarded a contract to operate a 4,500-[slot-machine](/wiki/Slot_machine "Slot machine") [racino](/wiki/Racino "Racino") at the [Aqueduct Race Track](/wiki/Aqueduct_Race_Track "Aqueduct Race Track"). Jay-Z became interested in the project after [New York Governor](/wiki/New_York_Governor "New York Governor") [David Paterson](/wiki/David_Paterson "David Paterson") who awarded the contract said there had to be an [affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action "Affirmative action") component to the ownership. Jay-Z was initially approached by casino mogul [Steve Wynn](/wiki/Steve_Wynn "Steve Wynn"), who was also bidding on the contract. On March 9, 2010, Jay-Z and Flake withdrew from the project, and Paterson [recused](/wiki/Recused "Recused") himself from further involvement.[[195]](#cite_note-196)[[196]](#cite_note-197)

### Media

On April 5, 2011, Jay-Z launched the popular culture and [lifestyle](/wiki/Lifestyle_magazine "Lifestyle magazine") online magazine *Life + Times*. It features content that showcases his high-end tastes in clothing, appliances, and cars. The site design is aesthetically aimed at the [upwardly mobile](/wiki/Upwardly_mobile "Upwardly mobile") young male demographic, with sports and music-related posts accompanying those about fashion and design.[[197]](#cite_note-198) Among the music content is the *Decoded* series, originating from Jay-Z's [memoir of the same name](/wiki/Decoded_(memoir) "Decoded (memoir)") and featuring a select rapper deciphering their own lyrics.[[198]](#cite_note-199)

In January 2015, after being contacted by the webzine, the DJ and radio host [Funkmaster Flex](/wiki/Funkmaster_Flex "Funkmaster Flex") revealed that he had been contacted in 2013 for a story about a digital app he made at the time, but alleged that the information was instead used to help launch the *Magna Carta* Samsung app for the release of Jay-Z's album of the same name. "But I was good with that. I ate that. Everybody's out here hustling", Flex reasoned to *[Vibe](/wiki/Vibe_(magazine) "Vibe (magazine)")*.[[199]](#cite_note-200)

In 2016, he signed a two-year exclusive film and TV Deal with [Weinstein Company](/wiki/Weinstein_Company "Weinstein Company") and with the deal gives them first-look options to create scripted and unscripted TV projects and film projects, and those projects were in works.[[200]](#cite_note-201) As part of his deal with Weinstein company, he produced a documentary series on the life of [Kalief Browder](/wiki/Kalief_Browder "Kalief Browder") who was imprisoned for three years and committed suicide upon his release. He also addressed [racial profiling](/wiki/Racial_profiling "Racial profiling") and [police brutality](/wiki/Police_brutality "Police brutality") in a conscious manner.[[201]](#cite_note-202)

### Other ventures

Jay-Z also co-owns the [40/40 Club](/wiki/40/40_Club_(venue) "40/40 Club (venue)"), an upscale sports bar that started in New York City, and has since expanded to [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City "Atlantic City") and [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"). In 2008, the 40/40 Club in [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas") was closed down and bought back by the hotel after attendance steadily declined.[[202]](#cite_note-203) In 2005, Jay-Z became an investor in [Carol's Daughter](/wiki/Carol%27s_Daughter "Carol's Daughter"), a line of beauty products, including products for hair, skin, and hands.[[203]](#cite_note-CNN-204)

In 2010, he announced plans to expand his 40/40 Club sports bar chain into as many as 20 airports, joining his Roc Nation business partners, husband and wife Juan and Desiree Perez, in a deal with [Delaware North](/wiki/Delaware_North "Delaware North").[[204]](#cite_note-205)

On November 16, 2010, Jay-Z published a [memoir](/wiki/Memoir "Memoir") entitled *Decoded.*[[205]](#cite_note-206)[[206]](#cite_note-207) The memoir was co-signed by [Dream Hampton](/wiki/Dream_Hampton "Dream Hampton").[[207]](#cite_note-208)

Parlux fragrances sued Jay-Z for $18 million for the failure of his cologne, *Gold*. They claim the cologne's failure is due to Jay-Z not doing social media posts and interviews about the cologne. Parlux claims they projected selling $15 million the first year, and $35 million the second, and subsequent years after the launch. The fragrance sold $14 million the first year and $6.1 million the second.[[208]](#cite_note-209) Parlux lost money on the venture and have had constant returns of unsold inventory.[[209]](#cite_note-210)

Jay-Z collaborated with [Cohiba](/wiki/Cohiba_(cigar_brand) "Cohiba (cigar brand)") to launch his own cigars.[[210]](#cite_note-211)

In August 2020, Jay-Z's [Roc Nation](/wiki/Roc_Nation "Roc Nation") partnered with [Brooklyn](/wiki/BROOKLYN,_New_York "BROOKLYN, New York")'s [Long Island University](/wiki/Long_Island_University "Long Island University") to establish the Roc Nation School of Music, Sports & Entertainment.[[211]](#cite_note-212)

In November 2020, it was announced that Jay-Z would be join TPCO Holding Corp., a newly formed cannabis products company, in the role of "Chief Visionary Officer".[[212]](#cite_note-213)

On December 5, 2022, Jay-Z and the Roc Nation announced their partnership with [Caesars Entertainment](/wiki/Caesars_Entertainment "Caesars Entertainment") to build and operate a [casino hotel](/wiki/Casino_hotel "Casino hotel") in Times Square.[[213]](#cite_note-214) The proposal was voted down by a community advisory committee in September 2025.[[214]](#cite_note-215)

## Personal life

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Beyonc%C3%A9_e_Jay-Z.jpg/250px-Beyonc%C3%A9_e_Jay-Z.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beyonc%C3%A9_e_Jay-Z.jpg)

Jay-Z and Beyoncé performing in November 2009

### Relationship with Beyoncé

In 2002, Jay-Z and singer [Beyoncé Knowles](/wiki/Beyonc%C3%A9_Knowles "Beyoncé Knowles") collaborated on the song "['03 Bonnie & Clyde](/wiki/%2703_Bonnie_%26_Clyde "'03 Bonnie & Clyde")". He also appeared on Beyoncé's hit single, "[Crazy in Love](/wiki/Crazy_in_Love "Crazy in Love")" the following year, as well as the song "That's How You Like It" from her debut album *[Dangerously in Love](/wiki/Dangerously_in_Love "Dangerously in Love")* (2003). On her second album, *[B'Day](/wiki/B%27Day "B'Day")*, he made appearances on the hits "[Déjà Vu](/wiki/D%C3%A9j%C3%A0_Vu_(Beyonc%C3%A9_song) "Déjà Vu (Beyoncé song)")" and "[Upgrade U](/wiki/Upgrade_U "Upgrade U")". In the video for the latter song, she comically imitates his appearance.[[215]](#cite_note-216) They kept a low profile while dating, and were married on April 4, 2008, in a private ceremony in [Tribeca](/wiki/Tribeca "Tribeca"), New York City.[[216]](#cite_note-peoplemarrige-217) Their relationship became a matter of public record on April 22, 2008,[[217]](#cite_note-218) although Beyoncé did not publicly debut her US$5 million [Lorraine Schwartz](/wiki/Lorraine_Schwartz "Lorraine Schwartz")-designed wedding ring until the Fashion Rocks concert on September 5, 2008.[[218]](#cite_note-219) Since their marriage, they generally avoid discussing their relationship,[[219]](#cite_note-220) and Beyoncé has stated her belief that this has helped them, while Jay-Z agreed in a *[People](/wiki/People_(magazine) "People (magazine)")* article that they do not "play with [their] relationship".[[220]](#cite_note-221)

In 2006, Beyoncé and Jay-Z were listed as the most powerful couple for *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine's 100 most influential people.[[221]](#cite_note-222) In January 2009, *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* ranked them as Hollywood's top-earning couple, with a combined total of $162 million.[[222]](#cite_note-Powerful_couple-223) They made it to the top of the list the following year, with a combined total of US$122 million between June 2008 and June 2009.[[222]](#cite_note-Powerful_couple-223)

At the [2011 MTV Video Music Awards](/wiki/2011_MTV_Video_Music_Awards "2011 MTV Video Music Awards"), Beyoncé revealed that she was pregnant with their first child.[[223]](#cite_note-224)[[224]](#cite_note-225) Their daughter, [Blue Ivy Carter](/wiki/Blue_Ivy_Carter "Blue Ivy Carter"), was born at New York's [Lenox Hill Hospital](/wiki/Lenox_Hill_Hospital "Lenox Hill Hospital") on January 7, 2012.[[225]](#cite_note-226) Jay-Z released "[Glory](/wiki/Glory_(Jay-Z_song) "Glory (Jay-Z song)")", a song dedicated to Blue Ivy, through his Life+Times website on January 9, 2012.[[226]](#cite_note-miscarriage-227) The song, produced by frequent collaborators [the Neptunes](/wiki/The_Neptunes "The Neptunes"), detailed the couple's pregnancy struggles, including a miscarriage Beyoncé had suffered.[[226]](#cite_note-miscarriage-227) Blue Ivy's cries were included at the end of the song, and she was officially credited on the song as "B.I.C"; she became the youngest person in *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* history (at two days old) to have a chart entry once "Glory" debuted at No. 74 on Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs.[[227]](#cite_note-bluebillboard-228)

On June 18, 2017, Beyoncé's father [Mathew Knowles](/wiki/Mathew_Knowles "Mathew Knowles") confirmed that she had given birth to twins with Jay-Z, a daughter named Rumi and a son named Sir.[[228]](#cite_note-229) In the following months, they purchased a US$88 million home in the [Bel Air](/wiki/Bel_Air,_Los_Angeles "Bel Air, Los Angeles") neighborhood of [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles").[[229]](#cite_note-230)

In 2023, the couple bought a house in [Malibu, California](/wiki/Malibu,_California "Malibu, California"), designed by the architect [Tadao Ando](/wiki/Tadao_Ando "Tadao Ando"), for $200 million. It established a record for the most expensive residence sold in California.[[230]](#cite_note-231)

## Legal issues and controversies

### 1999 Lance "Un" Rivera stabbing

On December 2, 1999, Jay-Z, who had come to believe that record executive [Lance "Un" Rivera](/wiki/Lance_Rivera "Lance Rivera") was behind the bootlegging of *Vol. 3...*, allegedly stabbed him at the Kit Kat Klub, a now-defunct night club in [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square"), New York City, during a release party for [Q-Tip](/wiki/Q-Tip_(rapper) "Q-Tip (rapper)")'s album *[Amplified](/wiki/Amplified_(Q-Tip_album) "Amplified (Q-Tip album)")*.[[231]](#cite_note-232) Jay-Z's associates at the party were accused of causing a commotion within the club, which Jay-Z allegedly used as cover while he stabbed Rivera in the stomach with a five-inch (127 mm) blade.[[232]](#cite_note-233) He surrendered to police the following evening and was placed under arrest, although he was soon released on $50,000 [bail](/wiki/Bail "Bail").[[233]](#cite_note-234)[[234]](#cite_note-235) When he was indicted in [Manhattan Criminal Court](/wiki/Manhattan_Criminal_Court "Manhattan Criminal Court") in late January 2000, he pleaded not guilty; he and his lawyers contended that they had witnesses and videotapes proving he had been nowhere near Rivera during the incident. Nevertheless, he later pleaded guilty to third-degree assault and accepted a three-year [probation](/wiki/Probation "Probation") sentence.[[235]](#cite_note-guiltyplea-236)

Jay-Z later addressed the case in his 2010 book *Decoded*:

> One night I went to Q-Tip's solo album release party and at some point in the night, I ran into the guy everyone's been telling me is behind the bootleg. So I approached him. When I told him what I suspected, to my surprise, he got real loud with me right there in the middle of the club. It was strange. We separated and I went over to the bar. I was sitting there like, "No the fuck this nigga did not..." I was talking to people, but I was really talking to myself out loud, just in a state of shock. Before I even realized what I was doing, I headed back over to him, but this time I was blacking out with anger. The next thing I knew, all hell had broken loose in the club. That night the guy went straight to the police and I was indicted. [...] There was no reason to put my life on the line, and the lives of everyone who depends on me, because of a momentary loss of control. [...] I vowed to never allow myself to be in a situation like that again.[[236]](#cite_note-237)

In 2023, Rivera stated that Jay-Z lied, and was not the one that stabbed him: "No. Jay-Z was not the guy that actually stabbed me that night (...) it's never been his history".[[237]](#cite_note-238)[[238]](#cite_note-239)[[239]](#cite_note-240) Rapper Nas, who feuded with Jay-Z in the early 2000s, had also claimed in his 2001 diss track "[Ether](/wiki/Ether_(song) "Ether (song)")" that Jay-Z was not Rivera's stabber. Rivera commented on this, stating, "The reality to it is Nas — and I don't know why didn't nobody believe him — on 'Ether' he talked about the incident. He let people know like, 'Yo, your man stabbed Un and you took the blame for it.'"[[240]](#cite_note-241)

### Civil lawsuits

In October 2024, an anonymous woman identifying as Jane Doe, with the assistance of hired attorneys Tony Buzbee, David Fortney, and Antigone Curis, filed a lawsuit in the Southern District of New York, accusing two celebrities of sexual assault at an MTV Video Music Awards after-party in 2000.[[241]](#cite_note-242)[[242]](#cite_note-243) A couple months after the initial filing, the lawsuit was amended to name Jay-Z as a defendant. Jay-Z denied the allegations and described it as a blackmail attempt.[[243]](#cite_note-244)

In December 2024, the accuser participated in an interview with [NBC News](/wiki/NBC_News "NBC News"), during which she acknowledged making "mistakes" in her recollection of certain details surrounding the alleged events, while maintaining that the core of her accusation was true.[[244]](#cite_note-245) According to the NBC News investigation, Doe's inconsistencies included her earlier claim that her father picked her up after the alleged assault, something her father says he does not recall as they lived five hours away from New York. She also claimed to have spoken with musician [Benji Madden](/wiki/Benji_Madden "Benji Madden") at the afterparty about his tattoos, but his representatives confirmed he was touring the midwest at the time and did not attend the VMAs.
