Additionally, photographs from that night showed Jay-Z and Combs in different after-parties, though the precise whereabouts of the rappers over the full time span remain unverified.[[245]](#cite_note-246)

On February 14, 2025, the lawsuit against Jay-Z was officially dropped. A filing in federal court in Manhattan stated that the case was voluntarily dismissed with prejudice, not allowing for a future refiling. The suit was dismissed without settlement.[[246]](#cite_note-247) Shortly thereafter, Jay-Z described the outcome as "a victory."[[247]](#cite_note-248)[[248]](#cite_note-249)

## Philanthropy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Jay-Z%2C_Gloria_Carter.jpg/250px-Jay-Z%2C_Gloria_Carter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jay-Z,_Gloria_Carter.jpg)

Jay-Z with his mother Gloria Carter in September 2011

During his first retirement from music,[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Jay-Z became involved in philanthropy. In 2003, along with his mother, Jay-Z founded the Shawn Carter Foundation, which assists eligible students facing socio-economic hardships attend and get through college.[[249]](#cite_note-250) In August 2006, he met with [Kofi Annan](/wiki/Kofi_Annan "Kofi Annan"), then [Secretary-General of the United Nations](/wiki/Secretary-General_of_the_United_Nations "Secretary-General of the United Nations") at the organization's New York City headquarters, where he pledged to use his upcoming world tour to raise awareness of the global [water shortage](/wiki/Water_shortage "Water shortage"), a challenge he learned about following a visit to Africa by [Bono](/wiki/Bono "Bono"), the lead singer of [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2").[[250]](#cite_note-251) The effort took place in partnership with the UN,[[251]](#cite_note-252) as well as MTV, which produced a documentary entitled *Diary of Jay-Z: Water for Life*, first airing in November 2006.[[252]](#cite_note-253) Along with [Sean Combs](/wiki/Sean_Combs "Sean Combs"), Jay-Z pledged $1 million to the [American Red Cross](/wiki/American_Red_Cross "American Red Cross")'s relief effort after [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina").[[253]](#cite_note-254) Jay-Z supported [Kanye West](/wiki/Kanye_West "Kanye West") after West's outburst against President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") during a [live Katrina charity telethon](/wiki/A_Concert_for_Hurricane_Relief "A Concert for Hurricane Relief").[[254]](#cite_note-255) He also addressed the [Katrina disaster](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina_disaster_relief "Hurricane Katrina disaster relief") and the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States")'s response in his one-verse song "[Minority Report](/wiki/Kingdom_Come_(Jay-Z_album) "Kingdom Come (Jay-Z album)")".[[255]](#cite_note-256)

Jay-Z has said that "the greatest form of giving is anonymous to anonymous". In 2013, author [dream hampton](/wiki/Dream_hampton "Dream hampton"), who co-wrote Jay-Z's book *[Decoded](#Further_reading)*, revealed that Jay-Z had established a trust fund for [Sean Bell](/wiki/Sean_Bell_shooting_incident "Sean Bell shooting incident")'s children.[[256]](#cite_note-257)

Jay-Z donated to bail out protestors arrested during 2015 activism against [police brutality](/wiki/Police_brutality "Police brutality").[[257]](#cite_note-258)[[258]](#cite_note-259)

In March 2020, Jay-Z donated $1 million through the Shawn Carter Foundation to aid in relief during the [COVID-19 pandemic in New York](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_York_(state) "COVID-19 pandemic in New York (state)").[[259]](#cite_note-260) In April 2020, along with [Meek Mill](/wiki/Meek_Mill "Meek Mill"), he donated over 100,000 face masks to U.S. prisons to assist in protecting prison inmates from [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19").[[260]](#cite_note-261)

## Political involvement

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Jay-Z%2C_Obama%2C_Bruce_Springsten_01_%288160159920%29.jpg/250px-Jay-Z%2C_Obama%2C_Bruce_Springsten_01_%288160159920%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jay-Z,_Obama,_Bruce_Springsten_01_(8160159920).jpg)

Jay-Z with [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") and [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen") at a rally in 2012

In 2006, Jay-Z appeared with [Russell Simmons](/wiki/Russell_Simmons "Russell Simmons") in a public service announcement denouncing racism and [antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism"), sponsored by the [Foundation for Ethnic Understanding](/wiki/Foundation_for_Ethnic_Understanding "Foundation for Ethnic Understanding").[[261]](#cite_note-262) In 2008, Jay-Z was involved in the [2008 United States presidential election](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election"), supporting enhanced voter participation.[[262]](#cite_note-263) He supported the 2008 presidential candidacy of [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") and performed voter-drive concerts financed by the Democrats' campaign.[[263]](#cite_note-264) He also became acquainted with Obama, who in June 2008 said, "Every time I talk to Jay-Z, who is a brilliant talent and a good guy, I enjoy how he thinks. That's somebody who is going to start branching out and can help shape attitudes in a real positive way."[[264]](#cite_note-265)

During the [2010 mid-term elections](/wiki/2010_United_States_elections "2010 United States elections"), Jay-Z appeared with other artists in a [HeadCount](/wiki/HeadCount "HeadCount") advertisement, urging voters, especially younger ones, to register and vote.[[265]](#cite_note-266) In May 2012, Jay-Z endorsed President Obama's support of [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage")[[266]](#cite_note-267) and participated in his re-election campaign.[[267]](#cite_note-268)

Musician and [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement") activist [Harry Belafonte](/wiki/Harry_Belafonte "Harry Belafonte") was critical of Jay-Z and Beyoncé for what he saw as their safe political stances, saying that they "have turned their back on social responsibility".[[268]](#cite_note-269)

In the [2016 U.S. presidential election](/wiki/2016_U.S._presidential_election "2016 U.S. presidential election"), Jay-Z and Beyoncé appeared at a [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton") rally in [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland").[[269]](#cite_note-270) Clinton praised Jay-Z for addressing racism, oppression, and the criminal justice system.[[270]](#cite_note-271) He described [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") as a "superbug" and condemned remarks he perceived as racist,[[271]](#cite_note-272) but later said that Trump's rhetoric forced people to come together and address difficult issues such as [white privilege](/wiki/White_privilege "White privilege").[[272]](#cite_note-273)

"The reason I did it was because in one-sixteenth cadence and your average four-fourth time signature beat, if you have more than sixteen syllables in a measure, all of the sudden, you're stuck with a dilemma to express yourself in a certain way or to get your point across the way you choose. I had to stuff those words, those extra syllables in the sixteen so it would transform those syllables into twenty-fourths, which became a triplet of an eighth. That's why I called it the triplet style."
